title: transitive Go CVEs (multiple) – request for rebuild / bugfix release
body:
Hi,

We are seeing multiple **transitive CVEs originating from Go dependencies** in Skopeo builds. These issues are not necessarily caused by Skopeo code itself, but by the Go toolchain and modules used at build time.

A rebuild with an updated Go version and/or updated `golang.org/x/*` dependencies should address them.

## Affected CVEs

Below are the identified CVEs with direct references:

- CVE-2026-46595  
  https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-46595  
  (Authorization bypass in `golang.org/x/crypto/ssh`) [1](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-46595)

- CVE-2026-39821  
  https://app.opencve.io/cve/CVE-2026-39821  
  (Privilege escalation via IDNA Punycode handling in `golang.org/x/net/idna`) [2](https://app.opencve.io/cve/CVE-2026-39821)

- CVE-2026-39829  
  https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-39829  
  (DoS via large RSA/DSA key parameters in `golang.org/x/crypto/ssh`) [3](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-39829)

- CVE-2026-39831  
  https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-39831  
  (Missing user presence check for FIDO/U2F keys) [4](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-39831)

- CVE-2026-39832  
  https://www.sentinelone.com/vulnerability-database/cve-2026-39832/  
  (SSH agent constraint stripping / auth bypass) [5](https://www.sentinelone.com/vulnerability-database/cve-2026-39832/)

- CVE-2026-39833  
  https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-39833  
  (Keyring constraint bypass in `golang.org/x/crypto/ssh/agent`) [6](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-39833)

- CVE-2026-39834  
- CVE-2026-39832  
- CVE-2026-39831  
- CVE-2026-39380  
- CVE-2026-42508  
- CVE-2026-46597  

(Note: these appear to be part of the same Go vulnerability batch affecting `golang.org/x/crypto` and related modules.)

## Context

Several of the above vulnerabilities impact Go libraries commonly used by Skopeo:

- `golang.org/x/crypto/ssh`
- `golang.org/x/crypto/ssh/agent`
- `golang.org/x/net/idna`

These vulnerabilities include:
- authentication and authorization bypasses [1](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-46595)[6](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-39833)  
- SSH key constraint enforcement issues [5](https://www.sentinelone.com/vulnerability-database/cve-2026-39832/)  
- privilege escalation via hostname validation flaws [2](https://app.opencve.io/cve/CVE-2026-39821)  
- denial-of-service vectors in cryptographic parsing [3](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-39829)  

## Expected remediation

Based on the Go advisories, these issues are resolved via:

- upgrading affected `golang.org/x/*` modules  
- and/or rebuilding with a Go toolchain that includes the fixes  

(Example: upgrading beyond affected module versions such as `x/crypto < 0.52.0` or `x/net < 0.55.0`) [7](https://pkg.go.dev/vuln/GO-2026-5023)[2](https://app.opencve.io/cve/CVE-2026-39821)  

## Request

Could you:

1. Confirm whether current Skopeo releases are affected
2. Update the Go toolchain / dependencies accordingly
3. Provide a **small bugfix / patch release** (e.g. X.Y.Z) that includes:
   - updated Go version
   - updated transitive dependencies
   - rebuilt binaries

A rebuild-only release would already mitigate these CVEs for downstream consumers.

## Why this matters

Many downstream consumers rely on Skopeo binaries distributed via:
- container images
- distro packages
- CI/CD pipelines

These environments flag CVEs even when they are transitive, so having an updated release helps reduce noise and maintain compliance baselines.

Thanks for taking a look.

comments.body:
Thanks for your report.

A significant majority of those vulnerabilities are for code that is not even included in Skopeo. We are not going to be spending time “fixing” those, nor “analyzing impact” of those.

Fix the “CVE” scanner implementation to stop reporting nonsense. And if you think a bug fix release should happen, make a specific case for a vulnerability + an exploitability path. (I’m not saying there isn’t one but we can’t be the first human line of filtering for you.)

@Thadir `rebuild` tells me this issue should be redirected to the distros / whoever ships the skopeo build you use. Upstream skopeo automatically updates dependencies via `renovate` so I don't see anything else to be done here. Closing..

Drive-by-comment:  I spent some time thinking about this problem space while working on images.redhat.com.  I totally empathize with the stance of upstreams stating that certain CVEs are not applicable and in some cases the code is not even vendored.

The sad truth is that the CVE system isn't always smart/detailed enough to cover that, so the only thing scanners can use is the SBOMs where they'll see the dependency getting vendored.

My honest recommendation is to cut regular patch releases upstream even if it only bumps dependencies.  While this may not improve security, it will certainly help the compliance problem of managing all the CVEs.

The thinking behind is that doing that will not only improve users' lives as they don't have to look at (bogus) CVEs but also the lives of upstream maintainers who won't use precious time explaining that a given CVE isn't applicable.  Changing the CVE system is an uphill battle IMO.

Below is a snippet of running grype against the latest skopeo image.  It's quite impossible for users to navigate this space.


> [@Thadir](https://github.com/Thadir) `rebuild` tells me this issue should be redirected to the distros / whoever ships the skopeo build you use. Upstream skopeo automatically updates dependencies via `renovate` so I don't see anything else to be done here. Closing..

I totaly understand, but if your in a nis2 enviorment I am already pulling your package directly and still hitting the cve as I am literly in my distor to not be part of the cve I do this:
```
# ── skopeo binary install ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Install skopeo from pre-built binary (lework/skopeo-binary — official
# containers/skopeo source) instead of the OS package.
# Rationale: OS-provided skopeo on UBI10 carries transitive CVEs (CVSS 10)
# inherited from system libraries; the pinned binary ships clean static builds.
# Update SKOPEO_VERSION + SHA256 hashes together on each upgrade.
ARG SKOPEO_VERSION=1.23.0
RUN set -eux; \
    ARCH="$(uname -m)"; \
    case "${ARCH}" in \
      x86_64)  ESUM='15696e068c02a163e20013fab79f40cdc6c8022d99e1aee8676f6d540404691a'; ARCH_SUFFIX='linux-amd64';  ;; \
      aarch64) ESUM='071ac4bf63c76d10de6dde23a28348671e9b1427123e966640782b5b68204223'; ARCH_SUFFIX='linux-arm64';  ;; \
      ppc64le) ESUM='177e3f9654d8226b0886a343e034071aab6b0d16d4a491b3706e73af992a0ec7'; ARCH_SUFFIX='linux-ppc64le'; ;; \
      *) echo "Unsupported arch: ${ARCH}"; exit 1; ;; \
    esac; \
    curl -fsSL -o /usr/local/bin/skopeo \
        "${MIRROR_GITHUB}/lework/skopeo-binary/releases/download/v${SKOPEO_VERSION}/skopeo-${ARCH_SUFFIX}"; \
    echo "${ESUM}  /usr/local/bin/skopeo" | sha256sum -c -; \
    chmod 755 /usr/local/bin/skopeo; \
    skopeo --version
```

> Drive-by-comment: I spent some time thinking about this problem space while working on images.redhat.com. I totally empathize with the stance of upstreams stating that certain CVEs are not applicable and in some cases the code is not even vendored.
> 
> The sad truth is that the CVE system isn't always smart/detailed enough to cover that, so the only thing scanners can use is the SBOMs where they'll see the dependency getting vendored.

The bureaucracies that mandate paying attention to “CVEs” (not even “vulnerabilities”) should be paying the full cost of that themselves. It’s unreasonable for a git repository “jump” whenever someone in the world decides that should happen; and the project will never be able to promise any sort of SLA anyway. (Various commercial support services do exist to help their customers navigate that.)

> My honest recommendation is to cut regular patch releases upstream even if it only bumps dependencies. While this may not improve security, it will certainly help the compliance problem of managing all the CVEs.

OTOH it adds operational overhead to users.

Either way this is all theoretical, the capacity to do these clearly non-essential things just isn’t here.

> I am already pulling your package directly
…
> ```
> # Install skopeo from pre-built binary (lework/skopeo-binary — official
> # containers/skopeo source) instead of the OS package.
> ```

To be explicit, the `lework/skopeo-binary` repository is not maintained by this project and we make no representations about it.

> The bureaucracies that mandate paying attention to “CVEs” (not even “vulnerabilities”) should be paying the full cost of that themselves.

Most importantly because that’s the only thing that will motivate them to improve the tooling to stop reporting all of the false positives.
